description:
'''Reggaeton''' ({{IPAc-en|UK|ˈ|r|ɛ|ɡ|eɪ|t|oʊ|n|,_|ˌ|r|ɛ|ɡ|eɪ|ˈ|t|ɒ|n}}, {{IPAc-en|US|ˌ|r|ɛ|ɡ|eɪ|ˈ|t|oʊ|n|,_|ˌ|r|eɪ|ɡ|-}}) is a style of [[popular music|popular]] [[dance music]] that originated in [[Puerto Rico]] from the [[Spanish reggae]] of [[Panamanian reggaetón|Panama]] during the late 1980s. It has been popularized and dominated by artists from Puerto Rico since the early 1990s. 

The culture surrounding the genre includes distinct dance styles, most notably "perreo", also known as [[sandungueo]], which features sensual movements heavily influenced by Jamaican [[dancehall]], [[Salsa music|salsa]] and [[Merengue music|merengue]] and another Latin rhythmic styles.

It evolved from [[dancehall]], with elements of [[Hip hop music|hip hop]], [[Latin American music|Latin American]], and [[Caribbean music]]. Vocals include [[Toasting (Jamaican music)|toasting]]/[[rapping]] and singing or [[rap-singing]], typically in Spanish. Reggaeton is regarded as one of the most popular music genres in the [[Caribbean Spanish|Spanish-speaking Caribbean]]. By the 2010s, the genre had seen increased popularity across Latin America, as well as acceptance within mainstream Western music.

While drawing early stylistic roots from reggae in Spanish and Jamaican dancehall, Puerto Rico served as the cultural and creative backbone of the genre.
